data.title: Accenture Targets AI And Network Intelligence To Reshape Long Term Growth
data.description:
• Accenture (NYSE:ACN) has entered a multi year collaboration with Mistral AI to co develop enterprise grade AI solutions for clients.
  • The company has also announced an agreement to acquire Ookla, a network intelligence and connectivity insights provider.
  • These moves add AI modeling capabilities and network performance data to Accenture's existing technology and consulting services.

Accenture is making these moves at a time when its share price sits at $209.89, with a 6.7% gain over the past week, a 20.4% decline over 30 days, and a 19.3% decline year to date. Over the past year, the stock shows a 37.9% decline, and over 3 and 5 years, returns of a 16.1% decline and a 10.6% decline respectively, signaling a challenging stretch for NYSE:ACN holders.

For you as an investor, the Mistral AI partnership and planned Ookla acquisition highlight where Accenture is choosing to invest its time and capital. How effectively the company turns these AI and network intelligence capabilities into real world client projects and long term contracts will be an important area to watch as you assess the role of NYSE:ACN in a broader portfolio.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Accenture by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Accenture.

We've flagged 0 risks for Accenture. See which could impact your investment.

For you as a shareholder, the Mistral AI collaboration and planned Ookla purchase point to Accenture doubling down on two areas clients are already asking about a lot: production grade AI and reliable connectivity. Mistral AI gives Accenture access to a focused suite of large language models that can be tailored to regional rules, including in Europe, which may matter for clients weighing data privacy and regulatory concerns. At the same time, bringing in Ookla’s Speedtest, Downdetector, Ekahau and RootMetrics data sets could help Accenture advise telecom operators, hyperscalers and large enterprises on how AI-powered tools sit on top of real world network performance rather than ideal lab conditions.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Accenture Narrative

  • The Mistral AI deal lines up with the existing narrative that partnerships and GenAI focused investments can support future revenue from large scale enterprise modernization projects.
  • Relying more on external AI partners such as Mistral AI, on top of OpenAI and Anthropic, could intensify execution risk if clients compare pricing and capabilities across Accenture and peers like IBM or Deloitte and push harder on fees.
  • The potential impact of network intelligence from Ookla on Accenture’s delivery quality, compared with consulting peers such as Capgemini or Cognizant, is not fully reflected in the existing narrative detail.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Accenture to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Growing dependence on external AI models and network data partners could pressure margins if licensing costs increase faster than Accenture can reprice client work.
  • ⚠️ Integrating Ookla’s products and aligning multiple AI partnerships at scale adds operational complexity that could weigh on project delivery if not managed carefully.
  • 🎁 Accenture is flagged as trading at 38.4% below one fair value estimate and at what is described as good value relative to peers and its industry.
  • 🎁 The company is identified as having an attractive and reliable dividend near 3.11%, with earnings growth over the past 5 years and forecasts pointing to further earnings growth.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, you may want to track how quickly Accenture turns the Mistral AI tie up into named client wins, particularly in Europe where data rules are front of mind, and whether those projects scale beyond pilots. For the Ookla deal, watch for concrete examples of combined offerings, such as AI-powered network planning or customer experience analytics for telecom operators, and how often those show up in client announcements. It is also worth keeping an eye on commentary in upcoming earnings calls about pricing, margins and any signs that AI related work is replacing, rather than just adding to, more traditional consulting projects.

data.title: Plug Power (PLUG) FY 2025 Loss Of US$846m Tests Bullish Margin Narratives
data.description:
Advertisement

Plug Power FY 2025 Results: Revenue Growth With Deepening Losses

Plug Power (PLUG) closed FY 2025 with Q4 revenue of US$225.2 million and a basic EPS loss of US$0.63, alongside net income excluding extraordinary items of a US$846.0 million loss. This puts a sharp spotlight on the trade off between scale and profitability. Over recent periods the company has seen revenue move from US$191.5 million in Q4 2024 to US$225.2 million in Q4 2025, while basic EPS shifted from a loss of US$1.48 to a loss of US$0.63. This sets up a results season where investors are watching whether higher sales can eventually support cleaner margins.

See our full analysis for Plug Power.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to set these results against the widely shared narratives around Plug Power's growth ambitions, risk profile and path to better margins to see which stories hold up and which start to crack.

See what the community is saying about Plug Power

Losses Stay Heavy At Over US$1.6b On A 12‑Month View

  • Over the last twelve months, Plug Power booked about US$709.9 million in revenue and a net loss of roughly US$1.6b, which works out to a basic EPS loss of US$1.42 on a trailing basis.
  • Consensus narrative talks about policy support and operational improvements helping margins, yet the trailing net loss of about US$1.6b against under US$1.0b of revenue keeps profitability firmly in the red, which means:
    • The idea of margin progress is running against the reality that profit growth comparisons are still not very meaningful while the company is loss making at this scale.
    • With EPS negative over the last year, the story analysts outline around margin improvement needs sustained follow through before it shows up clearly in per share outcomes.

Short Cash Runway And Dilution Keep Pressure On

  • The risk summary flags less than one year of cash runway and substantial shareholder dilution over the past year, alongside a share price of US$2.23 that has been more volatile than the wider US market in recent months.
  • Bears focus on cash burn and funding needs, and the trailing figures support those worries, because:
    • Running a loss of about US$1.6b over the last twelve months with under a year of cash coverage lines up with concerns that further external financing could be needed.
    • Substantial dilution already reported over the past year fits the cautious view that future capital raising could keep weighing on existing holders on a per share basis.
If you are trying to weigh those cash burn and dilution risks against your own expectations, it is worth seeing how skeptics frame the downside case in more detail. 🐻 Plug Power Bear Case

Premium P/S Multiple Relies On Revenue Growth Story

  • Plug Power is trading on a P/S ratio of about 4.4x, compared with a peer average around 1.3x and a US Electrical industry average near 2.5x. Analysts expect revenue to grow roughly 19.2% per year, faster than the US market forecast of about 10.2% per year.
  • Bulls lean on that faster revenue growth to justify the richer sales multiple, but the current numbers give a mixed read, because:
    • The latest trailing revenue of roughly US$709.9 million versus a P/S of 4.4x points to a market valuation that is already assuming the higher growth path plays out.
    • At the same time, forecasts still call for the company to remain unprofitable over the next three years, so investors backing the bullish view are effectively prioritising the top line story over near term earnings.

Approach 1: Carrier Global Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a business could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those cash flows back to today to get a single present value figure.

For Carrier Global, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections in US$. The latest twelve month free cash flow stands at about US$2.0b. Analyst and extrapolated estimates suggest free cash flow reaching around US$3.1b by 2029, with a series of annual projections in between that are discounted back using a required rate of return.

Combining these discounted cash flows, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of US$66.10 per share. Compared with the recent share price of US$61.17, this implies a discount of about 7.5%, which the model interprets as a modest gap between price and estimated value rather than a dramatic mismatch.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Approach 2: Carrier Global Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies like Carrier Global, the P/E ratio is a useful way to check how much you are paying for each dollar of earnings. It links the share price directly to the business’s current earnings power, which most investors watch closely.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends a lot on how the market views a company’s growth prospects and risk. Higher expected earnings growth or a business seen as more resilient can justify a higher P/E, while lower growth expectations or higher perceived risk usually point to a lower P/E.

Carrier Global is trading on a P/E of 35.14x, compared with an industry average of 23.00x for Building companies and a peer average of 30.82x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Carrier Global is 40.85x. data.title: Kosmos Energy (KOS) Deep Q4 EPS Loss Reinforces Bearish Profitability Narratives
data.description:
Kosmos Energy (KOS) has put a tough year on the tape, closing FY 2025 with Q4 revenue of US$294.9 million and a basic EPS loss of US$0.79 as net income excluding extra items came in at a loss of US$377.1 million. Over the trailing twelve months, the company has seen total revenue of about US$1.3 billion with a basic EPS loss of US$1.47 and a net loss excluding extra items of US$699.8 million, keeping the focus firmly on loss making operations rather than growth in the top line. With the stock trading at US$2.57 and margins under pressure, investors may frame this update around whether the current earnings weakness is acceptable in light of any potential rewards ahead.

See our full analysis for Kosmos Energy.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this latest earnings run matches or challenges the main stories investors already have in mind about Kosmos.

See what the community is saying about Kosmos Energy

Advertisement

TTM loss of US$699.8 million despite US$1.3b in revenue

  • Over the last twelve months, Kosmos generated about US$1.3b in revenue but recorded a net loss excluding extra items of US$699.8 million, with basic EPS at a loss of US$1.47.
  • Bulls point to plans for higher output and margin improvement, yet these TTM figures show the business currently running at a sizeable loss, which contrasts with optimistic expectations of future free cash flow growth.
    • Optimistic views highlight potential margin expansion from projects like LNG and lower unit costs, while the latest year still reflects a loss of nearly US$700 million.
    • Forecasts that profitability is not expected within the next three years sit beside this loss profile, so any bullish case has to work against a recent history of negative EPS.

Bulls argue that the current loss making picture could set up a sharper inflection if cash flows ramp as planned, but the TTM numbers make it important to understand exactly what upside they are counting on. 🐂 Kosmos Energy Bull Case

Low 1x P/S and 7.9% discount to DCF fair value

  • Kosmos shares trade on a P/S of about 1x compared with 6.1x for peers and 1.8x for the US Oil & Gas industry, and the current price of US$2.57 is around 7.9% below a DCF fair value of roughly US$2.79.
  • Supporters of the bullish view see this valuation discount as a potential opportunity, while the recent record of losses means the low multiple can also be read as the market pricing in execution and profitability risks.
    • Revenue growth of about 3.8% per year is modest against the broader US market at 10.2% per year, so the P/S gap is not obviously explained by faster top line expansion.
    • The company has reduced losses only gradually over five years at around 0.5% per year, which helps explain why the market may not be willing to pay peer level multiples yet.

EPS trend from small loss to deeper Q4 hit

  • Quarterly basic EPS moved from a small loss of US$0.01 in Q4 2024 to a loss of US$0.23 in Q1 2025, US$0.18 in Q2, US$0.26 in Q3, then a deeper loss of US$0.79 in Q4 2025 as net income excluding extra items for that quarter came to a loss of US$377.1 million.
  • Bears argue this pattern backs a cautious stance, and the recent data lines up with their concerns about ongoing earnings pressure and balance sheet risk rather than a quick turnaround.
    • The trailing twelve month basic EPS loss of US$1.47 and TTM net loss of US$699.8 million sit alongside views that the company is not expected to reach profitability within three years.
    • Higher share price volatility in the last three months and significant insider selling over that period add to the cautious argument that recent financial pressure is feeding into how insiders and the market treat the stock.

Skeptics often focus on this widening EPS loss and recent volatility as reasons to treat the current P/S discount carefully rather than assuming it will close quickly. 🐻 Kosmos Energy Bear Case
